name: PTSD Checklist for DSM-5
citation: Weathers, F. W., Litz, B. T., Keane, T. M., Palmieri, P. A., Marx, B. P., & Schnurr, P. P. (2013). The PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5). National Center for PTSD. Available from https://www.ptsd.va.gov/
validityNotes: The PCL-5 is a 20-item self-report measure of the 20 DSM-5 PTSD symptoms, developed by the National Center for PTSD (US Department of Veterans Affairs). Original validation showed strong internal consistency (α = .94 to .96), test-retest reliability, and convergent/discriminant validity (Blevins et al. 2015; Bovin et al. 2016). A total score cutoff of 31-33 indicates probable PTSD; 33 is the operational standard in most clinical settings. The PCL-5 is calibrated for past traumatic experience and cannot reliably distinguish post-traumatic patterns from responses to currently-unsafe situations (ongoing abuse, active threat); clinical interpretation must hold this distinction. The gold standard for diagnosis remains the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS-5).
instructions: These questions ask about a stressful or traumatic experience you may have been thinking about. Keeping that experience in mind, please indicate how much you have been bothered by each problem in the past month.

items.text: Repeated, disturbing, and unwanted memories of the stressful experience?
Repeated, disturbing dreams of the stressful experience?
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again (as if you were actually back there reliving it)?
Feeling very upset when something reminded you of the stressful experience?
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience (for example, heart pounding, trouble breathing, sweating)?
Avoiding memories, thoughts, or feelings related to the stressful experience?
Avoiding external reminders of the stressful experience (for example, people, places, conversations, activities, objects, or situations)?
Trouble remembering important parts of the stressful experience?

Having strong negative beliefs about yourself, other people, or the world (for example, having thoughts such as: I am bad, there is something seriously wrong with me, no one can be trusted, the world is completely dangerous)?
Blaming yourself or someone else for the stressful experience or what happened after it?
Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame?
Loss of interest in activities that you used to enjoy?
Feeling distant or cut off from other people?
Trouble experiencing positive feelings (for example, being unable to feel happiness or have loving feelings for people close to you)?
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively?
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm?
Being "superalert" or watchful or on guard?
Feeling jumpy or easily startled?
Having difficulty concentrating?
Trouble falling or staying asleep?

scoring.cutoffMeaning: A total score of 33 or above is indicative of probable PTSD and warrants further assessment by a qualified clinician. The PCL-5 is not a diagnostic instrument; the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS-5) is the gold standard for formal diagnosis.
